title: Whipped vanilla body butter
subtitle: A fluffy anhydrous body butter whipped from shea, mango butter, and fractionated coconut oil
excerpt: Whipped body butter has the appearance and texture of thick whipped cream. The whipping process incorporates air into the melted and cooled butter mixture, giving it a light, scoopable texture that melts quickly on contact with skin. Arrowroot powder prevents the greasy finish that can come from heavy butters.
sourceNotes: Whipped body butter formulation from standard natural cosmetics and homestead crafting references.

recipe.yieldDescription: 200 g whipped body butter (fills a 250 ml jar)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. A stand mixer handles large batches easily.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use once set. The texture is best at room temperature.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt butters gently, then refrigerate until beginning to solidify around the edges before whipping.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in a cool place. In very hot weather the butter may melt; store in the fridge. Discard if it smells rancid.

recipeTools.notes: Essential for whipping; hand mixing is not sufficient

glossaryTerms.definition: An anhydrous body product made by whipping cooled but not fully set butter and oil mixtures to incorporate air. The result is a lighter, more voluminous texture that applies more easily than solid butter.
A soft, creamy fat extracted from the kernel of the mango fruit. Rich in oleic and stearic acids, it has a light, non-greasy finish when applied to skin and a mild neutral scent.

Coconut oil from which the long-chain fatty acids have been removed, leaving mainly medium-chain fatty acids. It remains liquid at room temperature, has an indefinitely long shelf life, and absorbs without the waxy feel of solid coconut oil.
A fine, starchy powder used in anhydrous body products to absorb excess oil and reduce greasiness. Used at 2 to 5% of the formula weight.
A concentrated extract of vanilla in a carrier solvent, suitable for use in anhydrous cosmetic products. It adds a warm, sweet vanilla scent that is stable in oils and butters.

content.text: depends on cooling the butter mixture to exactly the right temperature before whipping. Too warm and the mixer cannot incorporate enough air; too cold and the mixture is solid. The refrigerator step brings it to the right consistency: softening around the edges with a solid centre.
prevents the product feeling greasy on the skin.
provides the warm scent. Shelf life is 12 months.

Melt shea butter and mango butter together in a double boiler until just liquid. Pour into a large mixing bowl and add the fractionated coconut oil. Stir to combine. Place in the refrigerator for 30 minutes, or until the mixture has solidified around the edges but remains soft in the centre.

Whip with a stand mixer using the whisk attachment on medium-high speed for 5 to 8 minutes until light, fluffy, and increased in volume. Add the arrowroot powder and vanilla oleoresin halfway through whipping. Continue until the texture resembles thick whipped cream. Spoon into the jar.

Store in a cool room. In temperatures above 25 degrees Celsius the butter may melt; refrigerate in summer. Shelf life is 12 months. The texture may change slightly with temperature fluctuations but does not spoil. Discard if the scent turns rancid.
